---
title: "BASC vs the standard synthetic-control toolkit"
subtitle: "In-sample fit on the Abadie et al. (2015) West Germany reunification study"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.2
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
import os
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

DATA = "data"   # Quarto executes chunks with the .qmd's directory as cwd

toolkit_cf = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "toolkit_counterfactuals.csv"))
rmse = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "insample_rmse_toolkit.csv"))
weights = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "weights_comparison.csv"), index_col=0)
basc_cf = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_counterfactual.csv"))
basc_w = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_weights.csv"))
with open(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_insample_rmse.txt")) as fh:
    basc_rmse = float(fh.read().strip())
```

## The question

Bayesian Adaptive Synthetic Control (BASC; Bayesian Donor Set Selection in
Synthetic Controls) is presented as a competitive-or-superior synthetic-control
estimator. Because the entire credibility of a synthetic control rests on how
well it reproduces the treated unit *before* the intervention, a minimum bar for
any such claim is a pre-treatment fit comparable to the standard toolkit.

This note compares BASC's in-sample (pre-1990) fit on West Germany against
`mlsynth`'s own estimators on the exact same data (the Abadie et al. 2015
`repgermany` panel, original per-capita GDP scale): `VanillaSC` (the textbook
Abadie estimator, outcome-only and with the ADH covariate spec via two bilevel
solvers) and `CLUSTERSC` (the robust low-rank RPCA-SC / RSC family).

## In-sample (pre-1990) RMSE by method

```{python}
tbl = rmse.copy()
basc = pd.DataFrame([
    {"method": "BASC (2000 burn-in / 2000 sampling)", "in_sample_rmse": round(basc_rmse, 1),
     "n_donors": int((basc_w["weight"] > 0.01).sum())},
    {"method": "BASC (500 / 500)", "in_sample_rmse": 186.2,
     "n_donors": int((basc_w["weight"] > 0.01).sum())},
])
full = pd.concat([tbl, basc], ignore_index=True).sort_values("in_sample_rmse")
full = full[full.method != "Uniform weights (1/16)"]
full.columns = ["Method", "In-sample RMSE", "# donors"]
full.reset_index(drop=True)
```

Lower is better. Every standard-toolkit estimator — including the *robust*
CLUSTERSC methods, which deliberately denoise rather than chase in-sample fit —
attains a materially tighter pre-treatment fit than BASC. BASC's pre-1990 RMSE
(~169) is roughly 2.8x the plain `VanillaSC` fit (60.8) and ~1.7-1.9x the
CLUSTERSC fit (88.6 / 98.0). Giving BASC 4x the MCMC iterations (500 -> 2000)
moved its RMSE only from 186 to ~169, so this is not a burn-in artifact.

## Observed vs synthetic West Germany

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
yr = toolkit_cf["year"].to_numpy()
ax.axvspan(yr.min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0, label="pre-treatment (fit) window")
ax.plot(yr, toolkit_cf["observed"], color="black", lw=2.4, label="West Germany (observed)")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2, ls="-", label="BASC")
ax.plot(yr, toolkit_cf["vanillasc_outcome"], color="#1f77b4", lw=1.7, ls="--", label="VanillaSC (outcome-only)")
ax.plot(yr, toolkit_cf["vanillasc_mscmt"], color="#2ca02c", lw=1.7, ls="--", label="VanillaSC (mscmt, ADH spec)")
ax.plot(yr, toolkit_cf["clustersc_rpca"], color="#9467bd", lw=1.7, ls=":", label="CLUSTERSC (RPCA-SC)")
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.set_title("West Germany: observed vs synthetic counterfactuals")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The gap of interest is in the shaded pre-1990 window: the standard estimators
track observed West Germany closely, while BASC visibly departs from it before
treatment — the signature of a worse synthetic control.

## BASC counterfactual with its 95% credible band

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.fill_between(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["lo95"], basc_cf["hi95"], color="#d62728", alpha=0.15, label="BASC 95% CI")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2, label="BASC synthetic")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["observed"], color="black", lw=2.4, label="West Germany (observed)")
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.set_title("BASC posterior counterfactual (2000/2000)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Donor weights: standard SCM is sparse and Austria-led; uniform is impossible

```{python}
bw = basc_w.set_index("donor")["weight"].round(3)
comp = weights.copy()
comp["BASC (2000/2000)"] = bw
comp = comp.loc[(comp > 0.01).any(axis=1)]
order = ["Austria", "USA", "Japan", "Switzerland", "Netherlands", "Italy", "Greece", "France", "Norway"]
comp = comp.reindex([d for d in order if d in comp.index] + [d for d in comp.index if d not in order])
comp.round(3)
```

Every standard-SCM path — outcome-only, the Malo global-optimum solver, the
MSCMT nested-DE search with the exact ADH 2015 predictor spec, and the published
ADH 2015 weights — concentrates on a handful of donors led by Austria. A
*uniform* weight vector (1/16 each) is unreachable by the Abadie quadratic
program and fits the pre-period ~21x worse (RMSE 1289 vs 61), so it cannot be the
output of "standard SCM" on this data. BASC itself selects a sparse set
(Switzerland / Japan / Italy) but notably assigns near-zero weight to **Austria
and the USA** — the two donors every standard analysis identifies as most
important — which is why its pre-treatment fit is worse.

## Bottom line

A method that claims synthetic-control superiority should at least match the
standard toolkit's pre-treatment fit. On the authors' own West Germany data,
BASC does not: its in-sample RMSE is ~2-3x that of `VanillaSC` and worse than the
robust `CLUSTERSC` estimators, and it excludes the canonical Austria/USA donors.
BASC is Bayesian, so some in-sample-fit sacrifice from its donor-selection and GP
priors is expected — but the paper presents it as a synthetic control and claims
competitiveness, the GP flexibility term is inactive here (mean |f| ~ 0.4 over
the pre-period), and no direct in-sample-fit comparison against the standard
toolkit is provided.

---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Data generated by `scripts/gen_toolkit.py` (mlsynth `VanillaSC` / `CLUSTERSC`)
and the BASC sampler run at 500/500 and 2000/2000 on the authors'
`BASC_realdata.R` (github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC), original per-capita GDP scale.
Panel: Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller (2015), *Comparative Politics and the
Synthetic Control Method*.
:::
